name: DJI Air 3 Drone with RC-N2 Remote
introText: The DJI Air 3 features dual main cameras — a wide 24mm and a 3x tele 70mm — both using 1/1.3-inch sensors, with 46-minute flight time, omnidirectional obstacle sensing, and DJI O4 transmission up to 20km.

specs.Cameras: Dual — 24mm f/1.7 wide + 70mm f/2.8 medium tele

whatWorks: Dual camera system at this price is unique — you can switch between the 24mm wide and 70mm tele in flight without landing or changing equipment. Both cameras use the same high-quality 1/1.3-inch sensor, so you don't sacrifice image quality when switching.

46-minute flight time is class-leading for a drone in this category — most comparable drones top out at 30-34 minutes. The extra time meaningfully reduces the number of battery swaps during a typical shoot.

DJI O4 transmission provides rock-solid video feed up to 20km with minimal interference — in real-world urban environments with WiFi congestion, O4 maintains a reliable connection where competitors lose signal.

APAS 5.0 omnidirectional obstacle avoidance detects objects in all directions simultaneously — much safer to fly in forests, around buildings, and in environments with moving obstacles than the tri-directional sensing on older models.
worthKnowing: 720g puts it in the Advanced RPAS category in Canada — flying the Air 3 requires an advanced drone pilot certificate from Transport Canada, which involves a written exam and a supervised flight review. This is a meaningful barrier vs the Mini 4 Pro (249g, basic rules only).
Registration required with Transport Canada — unlike sub-250g drones, the Air 3 must be registered, marked, and flown under advanced operations rules in most populated areas.
The 3x tele at 70mm is a medium telephoto, not a long lens — it's great for aerial portraits and cinematic shots but won't replace a 300mm for wildlife.
No built-in storage — you must use a microSD card; DJI's faster cards are recommended for 4K/60fps 10-bit recording.

commonComplaints.title: Requires Advanced RPAS certification in…
commonComplaints.body: Requires Advanced RPAS certification in Canada — the 720g weight triggers advanced pilot rules, requiring a Transport Canada exam and flight review before legal flight in most areas.

commonComplaints.title: More expensive than Mini 4 Pro…
commonComplaints.body: More expensive than Mini 4 Pro with less regulatory freedom — buyers who want to fly without certification barriers should consider the sub-250g Mini 4 Pro instead.

commonComplaints.title: No built-in storage requires microSD…
commonComplaints.body: No built-in storage requires a microSD card — at 4K/60fps 10-bit, you need a fast V60 or higher card to avoid dropped frames.

verdict: The Air 3 is the best drone for aerial videographers who want genuine dual-camera flexibility and the longest flight time available. The Advanced RPAS certification requirement is a real consideration for Canadian buyers — if you're not willing to get certified, the Mini 4 Pro is the better choice. For pilots who are already certified or willing to become certified, the Air 3's dual cameras and 46-minute endurance are worth the premium.

realBuyers.quote: The dual cameras change everything for aerial cinematography. I can get establishing wides and punchy tele shots in the same flight.
46 minutes is so much better than my old Mavic. I can do a full property shoot on one battery.
O4 signal is bulletproof. Flying in the city with tons of WiFi interference and I've never had a disconnect.

sources.title: DJI Air 3 vs Mini 4 Pro — Reddit (r/drones)

metaDescription: DJI Air 3 review for Canada: dual 1/1.3-inch cameras, 4K/60fps 10-bit, 46-min flight time, omnidirectional obstacle avoidance. ~$1,379 CAD on Amazon.ca.
